items.prompt_md: Right after popping `(d, node)` from the heap in Dijkstra's algorithm, what must you check before treating `d` as `node`'s final distance?
options: Whether `node` has already been finalized (is already a key in `dist`)
Whether `d` is negative
Whether the heap is now empty
Whether `node` equals the source
items.answer: Whether `node` has already been finalized (is already a key in `dist`)
items.explanation_md: The same node can be pushed onto the heap multiple times, once per relaxing edge. Only the first pop (the smallest `d`) is the true shortest distance; later, stale pops for the same node must be skipped.

items.prompt_md: Why can't you just use a plain FIFO queue (BFS) to find shortest paths once edge weights start to vary?
options: BFS's queue order reflects hop count, not true cumulative distance, so it can report a node as "closer" when it's actually more expensive
BFS uses too much memory for weighted graphs
BFS cannot be used on directed graphs
BFS requires every edge weight to equal zero
items.answer: BFS's queue order reflects hop count, not true cumulative distance, so it can report a node as "closer" when it's actually more expensive
items.explanation_md: BFS's ordering guarantee only holds when every edge costs the same. Once weights differ, "visited earliest" and "visited cheapest" stop being the same thing.

items.prompt_md: Why does Dijkstra's algorithm give wrong answers when a graph has negative edge weights?
options: Once a node is popped and finalized, Dijkstra never revisits it — but a later negative edge could still produce a cheaper route to it
The min-heap implementation cannot store negative numbers
Negative weights always make the graph disconnected
Dijkstra only works on undirected graphs
items.answer: Once a node is popped and finalized, Dijkstra never revisits it — but a later negative edge could still produce a cheaper route to it
items.explanation_md: Dijkstra's correctness relies on "pop order is distance order," which assumes adding more edges never makes a path cheaper. Negative edges break that assumption.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of Dijkstra's algorithm implemented with a binary heap on a graph with `V` vertices and `E` edges?
options: O(V + E)
O(V log V)
O((V + E) log V)
items.answer: O((V + E) log V)
items.explanation_md: Every edge can push once onto the heap (O(E) pushes) and every push/pop costs O(log V); each vertex is also popped once, giving O((V + E) log V) overall.

items.prompt_md: What does each entry pushed onto Dijkstra's heap represent?
options: A `(distance-so-far, node)` pair
A `(node, node)` edge pair only
Just the node id, ordered by insertion
A `(weight, source, destination)` triple for every edge in the graph
items.answer: A `(distance-so-far, node)` pair
items.explanation_md: The heap orders candidates by tentative distance so the cheapest unfinished node is always popped next; the node id is just the payload attached to that key.

items.prompt_md: Why can the same node end up in Dijkstra's heap more than once?
options: Because it gets pushed again every time one of its incoming edges is relaxed with a new tentative distance
Because the heap requires duplicate entries to sort correctly
Because `heapq` always inserts each element twice
Because the input graph itself contains duplicate nodes
items.answer: Because it gets pushed again every time one of its incoming edges is relaxed with a new tentative distance
items.explanation_md: A node can be reached from several different neighbors, each relaxation pushing a fresh candidate distance; only the smallest of those pops actually matters.

items.prompt_md: What guarantee makes it safe to treat a node's distance as final the very first time it's popped from Dijkstra's heap?
options: The heap always pops nodes in increasing order of tentative distance, so any not-yet-seen route to that node would have to be at least as expensive
The heap is emptied completely before any distance is trusted
Nodes are visited in the order they were originally pushed, like a FIFO queue
Every edge in the graph has the same weight
items.answer: The heap always pops nodes in increasing order of tentative distance, so any not-yet-seen route to that node would have to be at least as expensive
items.explanation_md: Because non-negative weights mean extending a path never makes it cheaper, whatever the heap would pop next can only be equal or more expensive than what was just popped.

items.prompt_md: What is the space complexity of Dijkstra's algorithm on a graph with `V` vertices and `E` edges?
options: O(V + E)
O(E log V)
items.answer: O(V + E)
items.explanation_md: The adjacency list takes O(V + E), and the heap plus the `dist` map each hold at most O(E) and O(V) entries respectively, so the total is O(V + E).

items.prompt_md: A buggy Dijkstra implementation omits the `if node in dist: continue` check after popping. What goes wrong?
options: A stale, more expensive heap entry for an already-finalized node can overwrite its correct distance with a worse one
The program crashes immediately with an index error
The heap silently drops the node and never processes it
Nothing changes — the check is purely cosmetic
items.answer: A stale, more expensive heap entry for an already-finalized node can overwrite its correct distance with a worse one
items.explanation_md: Without the skip, a later, larger pop for the same node would overwrite `dist[node]` with a worse value and re-relax its neighbors using that wrong number.

items.prompt_md: To reconstruct the actual shortest *path* (not just its length), what would you additionally track alongside `dist`?
options: A `parent`/`prev` map recording which node each node was reached from when its distance was finalized
Nothing extra — `dist` alone reconstructs the path
A second heap sorted by node id
The total number of edges in the graph
items.answer: A `parent`/`prev` map recording which node each node was reached from when its distance was finalized
items.explanation_md: Recording the predecessor at the moment a node's distance is finalized lets you walk backward from the target to the source afterward to rebuild the path.

items.prompt_md: You have a directed graph with non-negative edge weights and must find the shortest time for a signal to reach every node from one source. Which approach fits?
options: Run BFS, treating every edge as weight 1
Push `(0, source)` onto a min-heap and repeatedly pop the smallest tentative distance, relaxing that node's neighbors
Sort all edges by weight and union-find them into a spanning tree
Try every possible order of visiting nodes and keep the cheapest
items.answer: Push `(0, source)` onto a min-heap and repeatedly pop the smallest tentative distance, relaxing that node's neighbors
items.explanation_md: Weighted edges plus "shortest path from one source" is the fingerprint for Dijkstra: a min-heap frontier ordered by true cumulative distance, not hop count.

items.prompt_md: An unweighted graph (or one where every edge costs exactly 1) and you need the fewest hops from a source to a target. Which approach fits best?
options: Plain BFS from the source, since every edge already costs the same
Push `(0, source)` onto a min-heap and run Dijkstra's relaxation
Relax every edge for `k + 1` rounds (Bellman-Ford)
Sort edges and connect them with Union-Find
items.answer: Plain BFS from the source, since every edge already costs the same
items.explanation_md: BFS's queue order already reflects hop count when every edge costs 1 — reaching for a min-heap is unnecessary machinery for a problem with no real weights.

items.prompt_md: A weighted graph may contain negative edge weights (but no negative cycle), and you need shortest distances from a source to all nodes. Which approach fits?
options: Bellman-Ford: relax every edge repeatedly, since Dijkstra's finalize-on-pop guarantee breaks with negative edges
Dijkstra's algorithm with a min-heap
Prim's algorithm
Plain BFS
items.answer: Bellman-Ford: relax every edge repeatedly, since Dijkstra's finalize-on-pop guarantee breaks with negative edges
items.explanation_md: Negative edges are the trigger that rules out Dijkstra outright; Bellman-Ford's repeated full-edge relaxation still converges correctly.

items.prompt_md: You must connect every server in a network with minimum total cable cost — there's no single "source" server whose distance to others matters. Which approach fits?
options: Minimum spanning tree (Prim's or Kruskal's)
Dijkstra's algorithm from an arbitrary source
Bellman-Ford bounded to k hops
Plain DFS to find any spanning tree
items.answer: Minimum spanning tree (Prim's or Kruskal's)
items.explanation_md: "Connect everything as cheaply as possible" with no fixed source or destination is the MST fingerprint, not a shortest-path one.

items.prompt_md: A flight network has weighted edges, and you must find the cheapest price from a source to a destination using at most `k` stops. Which approach fits?
options: Relax every edge exactly `k + 1` times, snapshotting distances between rounds
Push `(0, source)` onto a min-heap and pop the smallest cumulative distance
BFS layer by layer, counting hops only
Try every ordering of intermediate airports
items.answer: Relax every edge exactly `k + 1` times, snapshotting distances between rounds
items.explanation_md: A hop/stop ceiling rules out Dijkstra, which has no notion of a budget; bounded Bellman-Ford relaxation answers exactly this question.

items.prompt_md: You need to determine whether all nodes in an undirected graph are reachable from each other (the graph is fully connected) — no weights involved. Which approach fits?
options: Plain BFS/DFS from any node, checking whether every node gets visited
Dijkstra's algorithm with a min-heap
Bellman-Ford relaxation
Prim's algorithm
items.answer: Plain BFS/DFS from any node, checking whether every node gets visited
items.explanation_md: Connectivity with no weights is a plain traversal question — reach for BFS/DFS, not a weighted shortest-path or MST algorithm.

items.prompt_md: A grid where moving into a cell costs its elevation value, and you must minimize the highest elevation you're ever forced to cross on the way to the exit. Which approach fits?
options: A min-heap of `(bottleneck-so-far, row, col)`, popping the smallest bottleneck and expanding to neighbors
Plain BFS treating every move as cost 1
Sort every cell by elevation and take the smallest n of them
Kruskal's algorithm over the grid's cells
items.answer: A min-heap of `(bottleneck-so-far, row, col)`, popping the smallest bottleneck and expanding to neighbors
items.explanation_md: A weighted grid where cost is baked into each cell is Dijkstra territory, just with `max` standing in for `+` as the combine rule.

items.prompt_md: You just need to know whether *any* path exists from node A to node B at all — cost and weight don't matter, only reachability. Which approach fits?
options: Plain BFS/DFS reachability check from A
Dijkstra's algorithm tracking cumulative weight
Prim's algorithm
Bounded Bellman-Ford relaxation
items.answer: Plain BFS/DFS reachability check from A
items.explanation_md: A yes/no reachability question needs nothing more than a traversal; pulling in a weighted shortest-path algorithm is solving a harder problem than was asked.

items.prompt_md: A weighted directed graph with strictly non-negative edges, and you only need the cheapest cost from a source to one specific target node (not every node). Which approach fits?
options: Dijkstra's algorithm, which can even stop early once the target is popped
Bellman-Ford, relaxing every edge for V - 1 rounds
Prim's algorithm
Try every path from source to target
items.answer: Dijkstra's algorithm, which can even stop early once the target is popped
items.explanation_md: Non-negative weights plus a single-source shortest-path question is still Dijkstra's fingerprint, whether you need one target's distance or all of them.

items.prompt_md: A large, sparse, directed graph where every edge weight is either zero or a positive number, and you need shortest distances from one source. Which approach fits?
options: Dijkstra's algorithm with a min-heap — zero is still non-negative
Bellman-Ford, since zero-weight edges are treated as if negative
Plain BFS, ignoring the weights
Kruskal's algorithm with Union-Find
items.answer: Dijkstra's algorithm with a min-heap — zero is still non-negative
items.explanation_md: Dijkstra's guarantee only requires weights to be non-negative; zero-weight edges never make a later path cheaper than an already-finalized one, so the algorithm stays correct.
